中東情勢をめぐる不安から円安が進んでいます。一時、外国為替市場で円相場は1ドル＝163円台をつけました。【写真で見る】iPhoneの日本国内販売価格引き上げ　「iPhone 17 Pro Max」は21万円超に　円安を反映か21日の外国為替市場で円売りが進み、円相場は一時、1ドル＝163円台をつけました。プラザ合意後に急速に円高が進んだ1986年12月以来、およそ39年半ぶりの円安水準です。

アメリカ軍が10日連続でイランを攻撃するなど、中東情勢が一段と悪化するとの警戒感から「有事のドル買い」が強まり、円安が進みました。

政府・日銀は円売りの動きを食い止めるため、円相場が1ドル＝160円台後半をつけた4月末から5月にかけて、11兆7000億円規模の為替介入に踏み切りました。

しかし、その効果はすでに帳消しとなり、介入前の水準を超えて円安が進んでいます。

title: 22日も各地で酷暑日予想 厳重警戒
body:
今日22日も危険な暑さに厳重な警戒が必要です。九州から東北にかけて、広い範囲で35℃以上の猛暑日、さらには岐阜市や名古屋市、甲府市、熊谷市などで最高気温40℃以上の「酷暑日」が予想されています。屋外での活動は控えたり、こまめな水分補給を心掛けたりするなど、万全の熱中症対策をしてお過ごしください。
昨日21日は岐阜県多治見市と郡上市八幡、愛知県豊田市で今年初めて最高気温40℃以上となる「酷暑日」を観測しました。今年から気象庁では最高気温40℃以上の日を「酷暑日」としています。

今日22日も危険な暑さとなるでしょう。岐阜市や名古屋市、甲府市では40℃、熊谷市では41℃の予想です。東海や関東甲信を中心に40℃前後まで気温が上がる所が続出しそうです。九州から東北にかけてのその他の地域でも、35℃以上の猛暑日となる所が多くなるでしょう。
今日22日は、熱中症警戒アラートが沖縄と九州から東北の42地点に発表されています。全国的に暑さ指数(WBGT)は「危険」レベルとなる所が多く、沖縄、九州から東北は熱中症の危険性が極めて高い見込みです。

明日23日も名古屋など愛知県内や岐阜県内などを中心に最高気温40℃以上の酷暑日が予想されています。危険な暑さはまだ続くでしょう。

屋外での長時間の活動や運動はできるだけ避け、エアコンを適切に使用するようにしてください。のどが渇く前にこまめに水分を補給し、大量に汗をかいた場合は塩分も補給しましょう。高齢者や子供は熱中症のリスクが高いため、周囲の人が積極的に声を掛け合うようにしてください。「酷暑日」は命に関わるレベルの暑さです。これまで以上に熱中症に警戒し、命を守る行動を心がけてください。

title: ウ軍総司令官解任 前国防相と対立
body:
ウクライナのゼレンスキー大統領（左）とシルスキー軍総司令官。ゼレンスキー氏が2025年8月26日、SNSに投稿した
ロシアの全面侵攻を受けるウクライナのシルスキー軍総司令官（60）が21日、ゼレンスキー大統領によって解任された。国民からの人気が高いフェドロウ国防相（35）との対立が公になり、国内各地でシルスキー氏に対する大規模な抗議デモが展開されていた。今回の決定には市民の憤りを沈静化させる狙いもあるとみられる。【写真】シルスキー司令官の解任を決めたゼレンスキー大統領総司令官の交代は2年5カ月ぶりで、後任にはドラパティ統合軍司令官（43）が就く。一部の前線でロシア軍を押し戻す動きが出ているなか、軍の改革と強化を進め、ロシアに侵攻断念を決断させられるほどの強い圧力をかけられるかが焦点となる。

　シルスキー氏は2022年2月の全面侵攻開始後、首都キーウの防衛や北東部ハルキウの奪還で評価された。24年2月に総司令官に就任すると、同年夏から始まったロシア南西部クルスク州への越境攻撃も指揮した。

　解任の背景には、15日に国防相ポストを外されたフェドロウ氏との確執がある。フェドロウ氏は16日の会見で、シルスキー氏が軍の改革を妨げていると非難。ゼレンスキー氏に総司令官の交代を進言したが断られたと説明した。

　フェドロウ氏を支持するウクライナ国民は、16日から連日抗議デモを実施。ゼレンスキー氏に直接怒りの矛先が向くのは、侵攻開始後の4年半で初めてだった。

　デモを呼びかけてきた退役軍人は、フェドロウ氏の復帰とシルスキー氏の解任を24日までに決断するよう訴えていた。

　事態を重く見たゼレンスキー氏は18日、両者とそれぞれ協議。「軍に関する決定がなされる」と予告するとともに、軍幹部らとの面談を重ねていた。（ベルリン=藤原学思）

title: 住宅全焼し4人遺体 千葉・八街市
body:
千葉・八街市で2階建て住宅が全焼　年齢・性別不明の4人の遺体発見
千葉県八街市の住宅で火事があり、焼け跡から年齢と性別がわからない4人の遺体が見つかりました。

　警察と消防によりますと、午前0時すぎ八街市沖渡の住宅で｢炎と煙がすごい｣と近くの住民から119番通報がありました。

　火はおよそ3時間半後に消し止められましたが、2階建ての住宅およそ130平方メートルが全焼し、焼け跡から年齢と性別がわからない4人の遺体が見つかりました。

　現場は、JR八街駅から2キロほど離れた、住宅などが立ち並ぶ場所です。

　警察は遺体の身元の特定を進めるとともに、出火の原因を調べています。

title: ニチレイ障害 ハッカー集団が声明
body:
ニチレイグループの物流センター＝17日、東京都大田区
サイバー攻撃を受けシステム障害を起こしたニチレイに対し「ランサムハウス」と呼ばれるハッカー集団が、インターネットのダークウェブ上に犯行声明を出したことが21日、セキュリティー関係者への取材で分かった。ランサムハウスは身代金要求型コンピューターウイルス「ランサムウエア」を常用することで知られる。【画像】ハッカーのイメージ　ニチレイに不正アクセス
声明を確認したセキュリティー会社S＆Jの三輪信雄社長によると、ランサムハウスは企業から顧客情報などを盗み、脅迫する手法が特徴だという。

　ランサムハウスは声明で機密データの流出を防ぐため、ニチレイに連絡するよう呼びかけた。

title: EU 中国系通販に制裁金1000億円
body:
ブリュッセルにある欧州連合（EU）本部前ではためくEU旗=2026年4月18日、西尾邦明撮影
欧州連合（EU）の行政を担う欧州委員会は20日、中国のアリババ集団が運営する海外向けネット通販「アリエクスプレス」に対し、デジタルサービス法（DSA）違反では過去最高の5億5千万ユーロ（約1022億円）の制裁金を科したと発表した。10月20日までに改善計画を出すように求めた。【写真】インターネットの通販サイトで「おもちゃ」として販売されていた拳銃日本では「アリエク」の略称で知られ、安さと幅広い品ぞろえで比較的若い世代に人気がある。

　欧州委は同社の2023年と24年のリスク評価などについて調べていた。「偽造品や安全性の低いおもちゃ、危険な化粧品など多くの違法商品がサイト上に流通しており、検出されても数週間にわたってオンライン上に残ったままだった」と指摘。「違法商品の拡散リスクを低減するための効果的な対策を講じていない」と断じた。

　また、違法な可能性のある商品を審査するためのスタッフを十分に確保しているかや、自社の推奨（レコメンド）機能や広告を通じた違法商品の拡散へのリスク評価も不十分だった。違法商品を販売する店舗は、罰則を受けても販売を続けられたという。

title: 中国 日本EEZ内での訓練巡り反論
body:
TBS NEWS DIG Powered by JNN
ロシアの艦艇と共同で航行していた中国の艦艇が日本のEEZ＝排他的経済水域の内側で射撃訓練を実施し、日本政府が申し入れを行ったことについて、中国側は「理にかなっていない」と反論しました。【反論する中国外務省報道官】日本政府の申し入れに「理にかなっていない」【写真を見る】中国外務省　林剣 報道官
「日本のいわゆる懸念は理にかなっておらず、中国はこれを断固として退けた」

中国外務省の報道官は日本政府の申し入れに対し、このように反論しました。

射撃訓練は沖ノ鳥島の南西180キロメートルのEEZ内で行われましたが、報道官は沖ノ鳥島について、「岩礁であって島ではなく、排他的経済水域を有することはできない」と主張。

そのうえで、「日本は他国の正当で合法的な行動を中傷し脅威をあおっている。国際社会は強く警戒しなければならない」と述べました。

title: 北外相が電撃訪露 さまざまな思惑
body:
北朝鮮の崔善姫（チェ・ソニ）外相がロシアを訪問し、プーチン大統領と会見した。記念行事などに伴うものではなく、詳細な日程や目的が伏せられた異例の訪露であるため、その真意に注目が集まっている。背景には、金正恩（キム・ジョンウン）朝鮮労働党総書記の訪露調整や軍事・経済協力の拡大、活発化する中朝関係とのバランス、さらには日米韓の連携に対する共同対抗など、さまざまな思惑があるとみられる。ここから見えるのは、安全保障協力を深める朝露関係と、中露を秤にかけて交渉力を高めようとする北朝鮮の思惑だ。
プーチン露大統領は19日、北朝鮮の崔善姫外相とモスクワで会談し、ロシアのウクライナ侵略を巡る連携や２国間の関係発展を確認出典：読売新聞オンライン　2026/7/20(月)
崔善姫外相がロシアを訪問したことを受け、金正恩国務委員長の訪ロが近く実現するとの見方が浮上している。出典：中央日報日本語版　2026/7/20(月)
北朝鮮を訪問している中国共産党序列4位の王滬寧・政治局常務委員と金正恩総書記が会談し、両国の関係を強化する方針を確認出典：TBS NEWS DIG Powered by JNN　2026/7/17(金)
（プーチン氏は）ロシア軍とともに戦闘に参加し、ロシアから国家勲章を授与された北朝鮮兵士について、「功績は決して忘れない」出典：FNNプライムオンライン（フジテレビ系）　2026/7/20(月)
崔善姫氏は19日にプーチン大統領と会見し、20日にはラブロフ外相との第3回朝露外相戦略対話に臨んだ。公開情報から確認できるのは「包括的戦略パートナーシップ条約」に基づく協力の履行や、国際情勢などについて意見を交わしたという大枠までだ。
その狙いとしてまず挙げられるのが金総書記の訪露調整だ。金総書記はこれまでロシア極東を訪れているがモスクワを公式訪問したことはない。将来の首脳会談について何らかの意見交換が進められたとも考えられる。
第二に、中露双方との関係を維持しようとする北朝鮮の思惑だ。崔善姫氏の訪露直前には、中国共産党序列4位の王滬寧・人民政治協商会議主席が訪朝し、金総書記と会見していた。この日程の近さから、北朝鮮が中国との関係改善をロシア側に説明し、朝露関係に変化がないことを伝えようとしたのではないか。
第三に、ロシアへの兵力派遣に対する見返りの問題がある。兵力や兵器の提供と引き換えに、ロシアから軍事技術やエネルギー、食料、外貨などを引き出す狙いは明白だ。
第四に、日米韓への共同対抗。両国は日米韓の安全保障協力を批判し、「多極化した国際秩序」を掲げており、こうした姿勢を改めて確認したとみられる。

title: 韓国 外交官全員の情報流出か
body:
【ソウル聯合ニュース】韓国の外交官全員の個人情報が流出したとみられるハッキング事件が発生した。国立外交院のオンライン教育システムがサイバー攻撃を受け、外交部本部の職員や政府機関から在外公館に派遣された職員などの最大1万件の個人情報が流出したと推定されている。外交部当局者はこのシステムについて、「約1万件のデータが保存されている」と述べた。　

　韓国の外交部は20日、正体不明のハッカーが昨年4～5月に同システムを乗っ取り、今年2月まで接続していたことが判明したと明らかにした。アクセス回数は公表しなかったが、同部当局者は「随時行われた」と述べた。

　同システムには職員の名前やメールアドレス、暗号化されたパスワードなどが入っており、職責や所属部署の情報もあった。住民登録番号や携帯電話番号、住所などのプライバシー性の高い個人情報はなかったという。

　政府は外交官や在外公館職員全体の名簿などを公開したことはないが、今回のサイバー攻撃で流出したとみられる。在外公館には外交官だけではなく、情報・安全保障機関の職員も勤務しており、これらの職員の情報も流出したとみられる。

　外交部当局者はシステムに保存されていたデータ全体が流出したと断言することは難しく、1万件というのは最大値を想定したものだとしつつ、「国家の安全保障と無関係ではない状況だと認識している」として、「（流出した情報に）外交部本部の職員はほぼ全員が含まれていると想定している」と語った。

　政府は北朝鮮や中国などのハッカー組織による攻撃の可能性も視野に入れているようだ。外交部当局者は「現在までに犯行主体を特定できる技術的な分析は不十分だ」とし、「他国などのハッカー組織を含め、あらゆる可能性を排除していない」と述べた。

　同システムは新型コロナウイルスが猛威を振るっていた2022年に作られた。これまで随時、セキュリティー点検が実施されてきた。外交部は「今回の攻撃はソフトウエアメーカーも当時認識していなかった未公開のセキュリティーの脆弱性を悪用してアクセスした後、正規のソフトウエア権限を利用したため、一般的な方法では検知が困難だった」と釈明した。

　現在、同システムは遮断された状態で、関係当局による調査が進められている。システム再開の可否や時期などは決まっていないという。

title: マンション1都3県 平均1億円超
body:
不動産経済研究所が21日発表した2026年上半期（1～6月）の首都圏（1都3県）の新築マンション1戸当たりの平均価格は、節目の1億円を上半期として初めて超えた。前年同期比13.1％上昇の1億135万円となり、2年連続で過去最高を更新した。人手不足や資材価格の上昇による建築費の高騰が影響した。【写真】「日本がゆるいから」巨大犯罪組織が目を付けたその“魅力”　アジア・マネーが不動産を爆買い…専門家が指摘する深刻な「不備」
エリア別では東京23区が9.1％上昇の1億4249万円だった。千葉県が56.8％上昇の8997万円、神奈川県が20.0％上昇の8346万円、23区を除いた東京が10.5％上昇の7550万円。埼玉県は1.3％下落の6469万円だった。

title: コメ価格下落基調続く 今後の鍵は
body:
コメ価格の下落が加速している。スーパーの平均価格は3週連続で下落し、業者間取引価格も統計開始以来最大の下げ幅を記録した。背景には、2025年産米の民間在庫が2過去最大水準まで積み上がり、米穀店でも前年産米が倉庫に残る異例の状況がある。こうした中、政府は備蓄米の買い戻し時期を「需給を見極めて判断する」としている。しかし、全国のJAでは8月中旬までに新米の概算金が決まる。価格形成が始まる前の今こそ、政府は買い戻し方針を明確に示すべき局面にある。
いまコメの価格が下落傾向となっている。背景には一体、何があるのか。現状を取材した。出典：FNNプライムオンライン　2026/7/21(火)
コメの在庫が積み上がる中、スーパーのコメ価格が3週連続で値下がりです。出典：FNNプライムオンライン（フジテレビ系）　2026/7/17(金)
業者間の取引の指標である6月の「相対取引価格」も史上最大の下げ幅となりました。出典：TBS NEWS DIG Powered by JNN　2026/7/17(金)
現在のコメ市場は、需給バランスの急速な変化に直面している。2026年産も前年並みの生産が見込まれることから、当面は価格下落基調が続く可能性が高い。備蓄米を買い戻しても、この流れを反転させることは難しい。しかし、市場から一定量を吸収することで急激な価格下落を和らげ、生産者や集荷業者の過度な値下げ競争を抑える効果は期待できる。例年、8月中旬頃に全国のJAが概算金を決定するため、それまでに政府が買い戻しを表明しなければ、「さらに価格は下がる」という見方が市場に広がり、新米価格が必要以上に下落する恐れがある。備蓄米の買い戻しは高値維持策ではなく、食料安全保障の観点から市場の混乱を防ぐ需給安定策として、今こそ決断すべき政策である。

title: 「着る虫よけ」人気 大手も参入
body:
蚊をブロック、虫よけ機能の服が人気　生地は防虫加工、通気性良く　紳士服大手も参入
今年は蚊の発生が例年より多いとされています。猛暑の影響で活動が11月まで長引くとみられる中、“着る虫よけ”が人気を集めています。【画像】虫よけ加工やUVカット、ドライ素材など、夏に求められる機能を備えたTシャツ
三連休の最終日で、関東の梅雨明けが発表された20日。大自然に囲まれた千葉県市川市のアスレチック施設は、多くの人でにぎわっていました。

　一番人気が、空中を疾走する全長60メートルの「スカイライダー」です。

　本格的な夏の到来。しかし、早くも多くの人を悩ませているのが蚊です。

来場者
「もう、ここに刺されてます。きょうですね、来てすぐですね」 
「私も刺されました。（虫よけスプレーは）汗で結局、意味ないですね」
「したけどね」

来場者
「（今年は）めっちゃ刺されました。数えきれないほど」

　森や池に囲まれているアスレチック場は、蚊が発生しやすい環境でもあります。施設では、3カ月ごとに行う殺虫剤の散布を今年はすでに2回実施するなど、対策を強化しています。

ありのみコース　丸山清史マネージャー
「このつり橋の下も結構ジャングルになってるんで、蚊が多いから、ここもたっぷりと薬をまきます。まかなかったら、瞬時にもうボコボコですね」

　専門家によると、実は今年は「蚊が多い」といいます。

（株）三共消毒　産業医科大学　非常勤講師
荻野和正博士
「去年よりは多い、明らかに。量的なものは、去年と比較で、例えば2割増しぐらい。最近の気候でいくと10月末までは確実にいる。条件によっては11月の初めまで発生」
こうした中、注目されているのが“着る虫よけ”です。

　「洋服の青山」がこの夏発売したのが、顔まで覆うことができる、パーカータイプの虫よけウェアです。

　蚊が嫌う成分を生地に加工し、通気性の高いメッシュ素材を採用しています。

　実際に着てみると、すごく軽くてとても動きやすく、匂いも特にしません。顔まで覆われているので、虫は入ってこなさそうです。

　この“着る虫よけ”、想定の1.5倍以上の売れ行きだといいます。

洋服の青山　新橋烏森口店
河口穂高上席店長
「ストレッチ、あとはウォッシャブル（洗える）、通気性。今まで培った、学んだものをしっかり、その商品に生かして作り上げた」

　虫よけ機能を備えた洋服は他にもあります。

Fishman　赤塚ケンイチ代表
「例えば、大きいビニール袋の中に蚊を1万匹ぐらい入れたとして、そうすると肌のほうにガンガン刺してくるんですけど、黒いTシャツのほうにはもちろん刺してこないくらい（防虫加工を）練りこんでいる」

　一見、普通のTシャツに見えますが、虫よけ加工やUVカット、ドライ素材など、夏に求められる機能を備えています。2万回洗濯しても効果は続くということです。
専門家は、普段の洋服選びを気を付けるだけで、蚊に刺されるリスクを減らせると話します。

荻野博士
「襟はないほうがいい。襟の所に（蚊が）隠れたり、隠れるもとをつくるので、丸首でゆったりとした白い服とかがいいと思います」

（2026年7月21日放送分より）

title: Zelensky sacks Ukraine's top army commander after days of protests
summary: Oleksandr Syrskyi is dismissed after backlash over the removal of popular Defence Minister Mykhailo Fedorov.
body:
Oleksandr Syrskyi was praised by President Zelensky for successfully defending Ukraine's capital Kyiv in 2022
Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky has dismissed the country's army commander-in-chief, Oleksandr Syrskyi.
It comes just days after the removal of popular Defence Minister Mykhailo Fedorov - a move that triggered street protests across the country.
Initial rumours that the end of Fedorov's tenure was related to a conflict between him and Syrskyi were soon confirmed.
Fedorov, 35, has been widely praised for energising the ministry and transforming the armed forces, as well as leading a drive against corruption. Syrskyi, 60, is seen by many in Ukraine as a Soviet-school commander reluctant to implement radical changes in the army.
He was replaced by Mykhailo Drapatyi, 43, who is a popular, battle-hardened general.
In a video post on social media late on Tuesday, Zelensky thanked Syrskyi for his military campaigns, including his role in successfully defending the capital Kyiv during the first days of Russia's full-scale invasion in 2022.
Zelensky also revealed that earlier in the day, he met Fedorov and offered him a "worthy position in power", but did not say whether the offer had been accepted.
Fedorov was last week replaced by Yevhenii Khmara, a former top official at Ukraine's Security Service (SBU).
"All decisions will be formalised tomorrow," Zelensky added in his post.
Mykhailo Fedorov has not said whether he accepted a new job offer from President Zelensky
Meanwhile, Fedorov congratulated Drapatyi with his new job, describing his appointment as a "new hope in the fight of free people for freedom and justice".
He said it was "the voice of change that could not be ignored".
Fedorov also revealed that he had called Syrskyi to thank him for the defence of the Kyiv region and other "historic battles".
"But we need to move even faster and write new chapters correcting all previous mistakes," he added.
Fedorov, 35, had been appointed defence minister only in January - but has been widely praised for using data to analyse and try to improve performance on the front line.
A former minister of digital transformation, he was active from the early days of the full-scale invasion by setting up a volunteer "IT Army of Ukraine" to launch cyber-attacks against Russia.
Later, he led a successful fundraising campaign called the Army of Drones and brought in elements of "gamification" to the war, designing a system that awarded Ukrainian military units with credits for hitting Russian assets.
Fedorov's ousting prompted six days of widespread protests, with Zelensky coming under mounting pressure to dismiss Syrskyi.
Zelensky appeared to have bowed to that pressure by replacing him with Drapatyi, who is seen as a younger, more innovative commander. Drapatyi is also thought to be a close friend of Federov's.
Federov's clear endorsement of Drapatyi is likely to go some way towards defusing the political crisis of the past week.
For the time being, acting Defence Minister Yevhen Khmara remains in the position. President Zelensky has asked him and Drapatyi to come up with a new defence strategy, covering key issues like reforming the military corps system, weapons supply and drones.
Syrskyi has so far not publicly commented on the latest developments.
On Monday, he wrote an op-ed for the Militarny website, saying of his relationship with Fedorov: "I perceived our relationship as a working one".
"If I offended anyone, Mykhailo Albertovych [Fedorov], I'm sorry."
On the protests that have been held in several Ukrainian cities, the general wrote that the issues of military contracts, procurement and mobilisation currently being aired in the press and boiled down to pithy slogans by protesters were a great deal more complicated than most people realised.
"Decisions on which people's lives depend are not made with slogans," he said. "Real work, not the public drama surrounding it, wins the war," Syrskyi wrote.
Protesters will still want to see Fedorov backing a position of real authority, where he can continue to drive forward innovation in Ukraine's battle with Russia.
If he gets such a position, Zelensky will have repaired the immediate damage. But many will not forgive him for making what they saw as the wrong choice in the first place.
Many Ukrainian soldiers outraged over removal of defence minister, troops tell BBC
‘Everyone's afraid to die’: Struggle to recruit soldiers in Ukraine turns violent

title: Lebanese army says troops deploying in 'pilot zone' after Israeli withdrawal
summary: A US-brokered agreement should see Israeli troops pulling out of a small part of Lebanon occupied during their conflict with Hezbollah.
body:
Lebanese army engineering units entered the village of Zawtar al-Gharbiyeh on Tuesday morning
Lebanon's army says its troops are deploying to a "pilot zone" in the south of the country following the withdrawal of Israeli forces under a US-brokered agreement reached last month.
Engineering units entered the village of Zawtar al-Gharbiyeh in Nabatieh district on Tuesday morning, the army command announced.
Later, it said Israeli forces had opened fire in the vicinity of the troops. Israel's military said warning shots had been fired after the troops crossed into the Israeli-declared "security zone".
The pilot zone plan should see Israeli troops pulling out of a small part of the territory occupied during their conflict with Hezbollah and the disarmament of the Iran-backed Lebanese group.
Tuesday's deployment began ahead of a meeting in Washington between US President Donald Trump and his Lebanese counterpart, Joseph Aoun.
Aoun was expected to urge Trump to put pressure on Israel to withdraw from the rest of southern Lebanon and support the Lebanese army in taking full control of areas where Hezbollah holds sway.
"I told him [Trump] I need two things from you: political support and supporting the LAF [Lebanese Armed Forces]," Aoun told reporters in the Oval Office.
Speaking alongside Aoun, Trump vowed that the US would provide help to Lebanon which had been "hit hard" for decades.
"It's been a very badly treated place and country, and we're going to have it properly treated," he said. "And treated with the respect it deserves."
The US state department announced on Monday afternoon that "pilot zone operations" had begun in Zawtar al-Gharbiyeh and two other Lebanese villages, Froun and Srifa, in line with the Trilateral Framework agreed by Israel, Lebanon and the US at the end of June.
It said the "milestone" was the direct result of discussions last week between Israeli and Lebanese representatives in Rome.
Later on Monday, an Israeli military official confirmed the Israel Defense Forces (IDF) would "adjust its force posture in one of the pilot areas" to allow the Lebanese army to carry out its mission, while also "safeguarding the security of IDF soldiers and preserving its right to self-defence and to remove threats".
"The objective of the pilot and the agreement is the disarmament of the Hezbollah terrorist organisation and the removal of its illegal weapons," the official said.
They also reiterated that the IDF would remain in the "security zone" - the 10km (six-mile) deep strip of occupied Lebanese territory running along the border - for "as long as necessary" to defend northern Israeli communities.
On Tuesday, the Lebanese army's command said its units had begun deploying to Zawtar al-Gharbiyeh "based on ongoing communications with the Military Co-ordination Group for Lebanon", referring to the trilateral body created to support implementation of the framework.
The statement also called on Lebanese citizens "not to head to the village until the security situation stabilises, and to comply with the instructions of the deployed military units for their own safety".
Later on Tuesday, the army command said Israeli forces had "opened fire in the vicinity of these units" and warned that the "attack hinders the implementation of deployment steps in the pilot zones".
The IDF said its soldiers had fired "warning shots into the air" after the Lebanese troops and an engineering vehicle "crossed approximately 150m (490ft) into the security zone and breached the barriers" in the neighbouring area of Zawtar al-Sharqiyeh.
The Lebanese troops "withdrew from the area, and no injuries were reported", it added.
The Lebanese army said its troops were already carrying out missions in nearby Froun and Srifa following Monday's US announcement.
An Israeli source also told Haaretz newspaper that there were no Israeli troops in those villages.
Also on Tuesday, Lebanon's state-run National News Agency said Israeli forces were behind two large explosions near the villages of Beit Yahoun and Kounine, in Bint Jbeil district. It also reported blasts from Israeli controlled demolitions in the town of Majdal Zoun, in Tyre district.
In areas still occupied by Israeli forces,many villages have been completely destroyed by Israeli strikes or demolitions.
The Israeli military says it has been destroying Hezbollah infrastructure, but human rights groups say some of what has happened there amounts to the deliberate destruction of civilian infrastructure, a possible war crime.
Hezbollah has rejected the agreement between Lebanon's government, Israel and the US, saying the provisions linking Israel's withdrawal to the group's disarmament crossed "all red lines". Israel has said it will not fully withdraw from the country until the group is fully disarmed.
Joseph Aoun is the first Lebanese head of state to visit the White House in nearly 17 years
According to Trump, the US has "concrete plans" to help Lebanon build the capacity to disarm Hezbollah, but he provided no details.
"They're also becoming much more self-sufficient," Trump said of the Lebanese army.
Additionally, Trump said he would speak directly to representatives of Hezbollah if requested by Aoun.
Lebanon was drawn into the war between Israel, the US and Iran on 2 March, when Hezbollah launched rockets into Israel in retaliation for a strike that killed Iran's supreme leader.
Israel responded by launching a bombing campaign across Lebanon and invading a significant part of the country's south.
More than 4,300 people have been killed in Lebanon, including almost 800 children, women and medics, according to the Lebanese health ministry.
Israeli authorities say 37 Israeli soldiers and four civilians have been killed on both sides of the border during the conflict.
Headteacher among four killed in deadliest Israeli attack on Lebanon since latest ceasefire
Israel strikes southern Lebanon as Hezbollah condemns new deal
Israel and Lebanon sign framework agreement after US-brokered talks

title: Carney looking at 'all options' as Trump announces 50% tariffs on Canada
summary: The duties mark a major escalation in trade tensions between the North American neighbours.
body:
Trump: 50% tariffs response to Canada's treatment of US farmers
US President Donald Trump has imposed a 50% tariff on a wide range of goods imported from Canada, in retaliation for what he called "unequal treatment" of US cars, dairy and alcohol.
Everyday consumer items like wine and hockey sticks, as well as industrial goods such as cement, are on the target list. Several key exports, though, will be spared, including energy, potash, critical minerals and fish.
Prime Minister Mark Carney said he had spoken to Trump and both leaders agreed to "intensify" trade talks in a bid to calm growing trade tensions between the North American neighbours.
But he warned "all options" were on the table in terms of how Canada could respond to the fresh tariff threat.
Since the tariff war broke out more than 18 months ago, Carney said, Canadians have "stood together" by buying home-produced goods and agreeing new trade routes with other countries.
"[The] country is stronger, stronger because we are united, and we will remain united," he told reporters.
Tensions between the major trading partners have been simmering since Trump returned to office in January last year and unleashed a wide-ranging global programme of tariffs, sometimes to pursue objectives not directly linked to trade.
Tariffs are taxes on imported goods that are paid to the government by companies bringing in the foreign products.
Asked about the latest tariffs, which the White House says will take effect in 30 days, on Tuesday, President Trump said Canada had been "very, very tough on us over the years" and  "no other presidents done anything about it".
"I love Canada, I love the people of Canada, many friends that live in Canada," he said. "Without us, there's no way they can survive."
The latest move by Trump comes after the US Supreme Court ruled earlier this year that many of the tariffs imposed globally under emergency powers were illegally enacted. The president hassince sought other legal avenues to push through his flagship trade policy, as seen in Monday's announcement.
Trump has said the levies will boost American manufacturing and jobs, but some economists have warned that higher charges on imported goods risks pushing up prices for consumers.
This new round of duties on Canadian goods has alsofuelled speculation about a potential wider tariff announcement by the US on countries across the world. US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer said on Tuesday that he expected to "see some action soon", when asked on CNBC about the possibility of forthcoming levies.
Canada, which is one of the US's closest trading partners, was one of the few countries to retaliate last year against Trump's tariffs, placing a 25% levy of its own on about C$30bn (£16bn; $21.7bn) worth of US goods.
Carney later dropped some of them.
The new US tariffs add to trade barriers already in place between the two nations.
The US has been maintaining active tariffs ranging from 15% to 50% on Canadian steel, aluminium and copper. It also charges a 35% tariff on Canadian softwood lumber, alongside a 25% tax on non-US parts in cars.
Meanwhile Canada has its own 25% counter-tariff on selected imports of American steel, aluminium and vehicles.
A White House fact sheet said the new tariffs announced this week applied regardless of whether the product was included under the existing free trade agreement between Canada, the US and Mexico, known as the USMCA.
But Carney said the move was the "latest in a series of unilateral US trade actions that began with the US imposing a series of tariffs in direct violation" of the USMCA.
The prime minister also cited "threats to Canadian sovereignty", possibly in reference to Trump's repeated calls to make America's northern neighbour the 51st US state.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford said that if the US goes ahead with its latest threat, "Canada should respond tariff for tariff, dollar for dollar".
The BBC has contacted the White House and the Canadian government for further comment.
Canadian wildfires rage as Trump warns Carney again over 'poisoning' US air
US blocks long-term renewal of North American trade deal
US announces new tariffs over forced labour concerns
As part of his executive action announcing the new tariffs, Trump issued three proclamations, relating to to cars, dairy products and alcohol.
Cars: Trump's proclamation pointed to Canada charging a tax on imports of US motor vehicles and parts that are not covered under USMCA. He argued it was "unreasonable" and that Canada has discriminated against the US by not charging other countries a similar tax. Automotive manufacturing in North America is highly integrated between Canada, the US and Mexico.
Dairy: The administration says Canada has taken protectionist measures on cheese, discriminating against US producers and putting the country as a whole at a disadvantage.  In Canada, dairy products that exceed its importation limits are charged a tariff up to 300%.
Alcohol: The boycott of US alcoholic drinks by most Canadian provinces has become a major sore point for the Americans since it started last year, but Canadian premiers have said that the boycott will be lifted if the US removes its tariffs on key Canadian sectors, including metals and automobiles.
Monday's announcement also comes in the wake of President Trump's threat to impose tariffs over Canadian wildfire smoke drifting into US cities, though this issue has not been officially mentioned as the justification for the move.
Earlier this year, the US chose not to renew the USMCA in its current form - while Canada and Mexico sought a renewal - as it aimed to make changes to the agreement, which was reached during Trump's first term in office.
Despite the recent US action, the treaty will continue to govern North American trade over the next decade on a rolling basis, but it will require annual reviews.
The recently announced tariffs highlight the Trump administration's opposition to USMCA, said Michael Devereux, a professor of economics at the University of British Columbia.
"It is a significant escalation because it directly targets goods that were previously exempt under the US, Canada, Mexico trade agreement that President Trump negotiated and signed himself in 2018," he added.
In February, the US Supreme Court struck down sweeping international tariffs imposed by Trump through the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) of 1977, saying he had exceeded his authority under a law reserved for national emergencies.
The White House then vowed to invoke other mechanisms to impose import taxes, and in recent week has announced fresh tariffs on a range of countries under a different law commonly called "Section 301". That included a new levy on Brazil, which has vowed to retaliate.
What tariffs has Trump introduced and why?

title: Dozens hurt in Bologna protests after man dies while being restrained by police
summary: Thousands of people took to the streets demanding justice for a Moroccan-born man who died on Sunday.
body:
Prosecutors have opened an investigation into manslaughter after the man's death in Bologna
Protests have taken place in Italy after the death of a Moroccan-born man while being forcibly restrained by two police officers in the northern city of Bologna.
Several thousand people took to the streets on Monday night demanding "justice for Abderrahim Fakir".
A smaller group then splintered off and clashed with police into the night. Prosecutors say 64 police were hurt in the unrest.
Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni said it was imperative that the truth surrounding his death should be investigated but "nothing can justify violence against law enforcement".
Police had been called on Sunday morning after a neighbour witnessed Fakir "behaving erratically" and kicking garage doors in the working class neighbourhood of Pilastro. The officers tried to calm the situation but then used pepper spray.
A video circulating on Italian media shows two policemen sitting on Fakir as he lies  face down and cries out for them to stop. A third person who is not a policeman pins his legs down as emergency workers stand by.
About two minutes into the video, Fakir stops struggling. The police officers tie his wrists and ankles and, when one of them realises Fakir is no longer moving, slaps him twice on the cheek. By the time an ambulance worker approaches, it is clear Fakir is unconscious.
Prosecutors in Bologna have opened an investigation into manslaughter against the police officers and the medical workers who were present at the scene.
Several eyewitnesses told Italian media that while Fakir appeared to be in distress he did not threaten anybody and was clearly unarmed.
"You can't die like this," his sister Khadija told Italian media. "They killed him mercilessly, and I want the truth."
Bologna mayor Matteo Lepore told Italy's La Repubblica newspaper that anyone who watched the video was "shocked".
He called for the demonstration in central Bologna on Monday evening and invited Fakir's niece to join him on stage.
"He was my uncle. He wasn't a number, he wasn't a news line... he was loved," Yossra Fakir said. "He deserved to be helped and heard, and he deserved dignity."
While the main demonstration on Monday night was peaceful, about 100 protesters clashed with police into the night and set two cars alight.
Lepore said on Tuesday he had seen "two different squares" in Bologna - "thousands of people gathered peacefully and spontaneously to express their support for Abderrahim Fakir's family" and a smaller group "who think they can respond to violence with violence".
Domenico Pianese, the Secretary-General of the police union, criticised Lepore's decision to call for a demonstration that later descended into chaos.
Calling for a protest "the day after a dramatic event like this one," Pianese said, was like calling "all the pseudo-anarchists and those who are anti-police... to take advantage and protest against the state".
Asked about what police got wrong when they detained Fakir, he said: "Judging from the images we saw, the intervention was carried out correctly."
Pianese argued that the fact the two officers had to use pepper spray and needed the help of a civilian to restrain Fakir showed that the use of force had not been excessive. "The first thing they did [after the incident] was... hand all [bodycam] footage to prosecutors," he said.
About 100 protesters ended up clashing with police setting two cars alight
Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni called the events "unacceptable".
While Fakir's death should be "thoroughly investigated," she said, "nothing can justify violence against police."
"Those who chose to use this incident as a pretext to set the city ablaze, attack officers, and sow violence... were not seeking the truth: they were seeking confrontation," she said.
According to Italian media Fakir's family arrived in Italy over three decades ago and had been living near Bologna since, where Fakir ran a small logistics and delivery company.
The lawyer who was handling his residency permit request, Barbara Spinelli, wrote on Facebook: "[Fakir] was a hardworker, he was kind... Ask yourselves why he died in the hands of those who had an institutional duty to protect his life."
Some of the signs at the Bologna protest referenced other infamous cases of people who have died in police custody, like that of Stefano Cucchi, who died in 2009 after being held by police for six days.
His sister Ilaria Cucchi, now a senator and member of the opposition Greens and Left Alliance party, criticised the government for the "solidarity" expressed to police.
Others on the opposition benches too criticised the government's backing of the police.
"If the right wants to import the ICE model, this is the wrong country for it," Francesco Boccia of the centre-left Democratic Party (PD) told the media.

title: US launches fresh strikes on Iran, as Trump warns of retaliation for deaths of soldiers
summary: Iran says it has hit two ships in the Strait of Hormuz as well as targets in Bahrain and Jordan.
body:
The US military says it launched another round of strikes against Iran to "further degrade" its capabilities to attack ships in the Strait of Hormuz.
The 10th consecutive night of strikes took place after US President Donald Trump vowed to punish Iran for the deaths of US soldiers.
In response, Iran saidit struck two oil tankers in the waterway, as well as US military assets in Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan, state news agency IRNA reported.
President Masoud Pezeshkian said Iran was now fighting a "full-scale war" with the US. Trump earlier said Iran had been hit "very hard" the previous night "in honour" of three US soldiers killed recently - two in Jordan and one in Iraq.
US Central Command (Centcom) said its latest strikes had hit Iranian "military command centres, maritime capabilities, missile and drone launch sites, and air defence systems".
Explosions were reported near the north-western city of Tabriz, where state media said one person was killed and several wounded, as well at the Gulf ports of Bandar Mahshahr and Bandar Imam Khomeini and the south-eastern ports of Chabahar and Konarak.
Centcom also insisted that commercial vessels were continuing to pass through the Strait of Hormuz.
Iran meanwhile said its forces struck two oil tankers as they attempted to transit through what it called the strait's "unsafe and hazardous southern route" and rescue teams were picking up their crews, according to state media.
The United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations agency (UKMTO) said a tanker in the Strait of Hormuz had reported being struck by an unknown projectile, forcing its crew to abandon the vessel and board a lifeboat.
It was not clear whether the tanker reported by UKMTO was among those cited by Iran.
Six fuel tankers and cargo ships crossed the Strait of Hormuz on Monday, according to preliminary data from maritime intelligence firm Kpler. Two of these vessels had previously been sanctioned by the US for links with Iran.
This is down from eight vessels crossing on 19 July, the data shows. All but one of the crossings made on 20 July were via the northern route near Iran.
Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) also claimed to have targeted a data centre belonging to the US company Amazon in Bahrain, according to state media on Tuesday.
The IRGC "attacked and destroyed the central data infrastructure of the American company Amazon in Bahrain with several cruise missiles," state news agency IRNA reported.
Jordan said on Tuesday that it had intercepted five drones after saying it had shot down three missiles late on Monday. Bahrain said drones launched by Iran had targeted its air navigation systems - but traffic remained unaffected. Kuwait's armed forces also said they had intercepted Iranian drones.
Later on Tuesday Kuwait said Iran had hit several power and water desalination plants the previous day.
At the same time, Tehran said diplomatic messages were still being exchanged by both sides through intermediaries, despite a collapse of a ceasefire.
Speaking in the Oval Office on Tuesday, Trump insisted Iran wants to "desperately meet", but that "until they're ready to meet in a meaningful way, we have no interest".
Trump added that the US would "heavily" strike Mount Kolang Gaz La - also known as Pickaxe Mountain - following reports that Iran may have moved nuclear centrifuges to the area.
"It doesn't mean anything unless they have the [nuclear] material," he added.
On Monday night the Department of Defense confirmed the identities of two of the US soldiers – Pte Isabella Gonzales, 19, from Texas, and Lt Tyler James Feehan, 25, from Hawaii - who were killed in an Iranian strike on the Muwaffaq Salti base in Jordan over the weekend.
Centcom said earlier a third service member also went missing in action after the attack, and unidentified remains were later found in the area.
(With additional reporting by Bernd Debusmann Jr at the White House)

title: VAT to be cut from household electricity bills in October
summary: Homes will save around £45 a year through the cut announced on Andy Burnham's first full day as prime minister.
body:
VAT will be cut from household electricity bills, the government has announced, as part of new Prime Minister Andy Burnham's pledge to help with the cost of living.
The reduction from 5% VAT to zero will come into effect on 1 October, saving a typical household about £45 a year.
Ministers said it would be funded by savings from the cancellation of the digital ID programme, which was going to cost £1.8bn over the next three years. The government estimated it will cost £850m this financial year.
But Labour's Darren Jones, who was sacked as chief secretary to the prime minister on Monday, accused the government of announcing an unfunded tax cut.
What does an Andy Burnham-led government mean for your money?
Three reasons why UK electricity prices are so high
Jonathan Reynolds, the new business secretary, said the cut would give people some "breathing space".
He said it was funded until "the end of the financial year" in March 2027 and any changes beyond that would have to be announced in the next Budget.
Suppliers have been told the VAT reduction should be passed on to all household customers, including those on fixed tariffs, as was the case when some charges were taken off bills in April.
The cut will take effect in England, Scotland and Wales, but equivalent funding would be given to Northern Ireland, which is regulated differently, the government said.
The reductioncould not be automatically be appliedin Northern Ireland due to EU rules limiting the range of goods which can be sold without VAT. Following Brexit, England, Scotland and Wales are not bound by these restrictions.
Small businesses who qualify for the domestic energy VAT relief and are not registered for VAT, as well as charities and residential care homes eligible for the reduced rate, will also benefit.
Cutting VAT is a relatively basic tool to reduce an energy bill - with larger households likely to save more as they use more electricity.
However, some vulnerable households are also high electricity users, such as for running medical equipment.
Jones, who was also previously chief secretary to the Treasury,wrote on X,externalthat the Digital ID programme was unfunded.
While praising the VAT cut as "good", he said "the government will have to set out how it will pay for its new policies at the budget".
Conservative Shadow Chancellor Mel Stride also criticised the use of the Digital ID budget to fund the VAT cut.
"The cuts to the Digital ID card budget are not real because the money was never provided in the first place.
"We are only one day in and already it is smoke and mirrors on the public finances."
Reynolds told the BBC's Today programme a VAT cut to electricity bills would be funded through a "straightforward switch spend" redirecting money for digital ID.
He said money for digital ID "would have had to have been found" and that scrapping the scheme removed "pressure" from the budget allowing the government to "give real help to people".
But the Office for Budget Responsibility said last November that digital ID would have to paid for out of savings from government departments and these had not yet been identified.
The spending watchdog said bringing in Digital ID had "provisionally forecast to cost £1.8bn in total over the next three years".
Government sources have insisted that departments have been working to find savings to fund Digital ID since the OBR made its forecast in November and this money would now be reallocated to fund the VAT cut.
Household energy prices rose by 13% for millions of people in England, Scotland and Wales at the start of July, under regulator Ofgem's price cap.
Price rises were driven by the higher cost of gas, but have a relatively limited impact owing to warm weather and lower energy use during the summer months.
However, higher energy prices caused by the US-Israeli war with Iran, which has constrained global supplies of oil and liquified natural gas, are likely to persist into the winter, according to analysts.
The VAT cut only applies to the current financial year — any decision to keep it in future years would have to be made in a Budget.
The government expects it will lower inflation by 0.1 percentage points.
In his first speech as the UK's prime minister, Burnhamvowed to provide "breathing space" for householdsstruggling with the cost of living.
On Tuesday, he said the VAT cut would "put more money in people's pockets".
The VAT cut is the second intervention on energy bills by the government in six months.
Former chancellor Rachel Reeves removed one levy and shifted others onto general taxation to lower bills in April.
Speaking to the BBC, Liberal Democrat leader Sir Ed Davey said: "I think we'll have a summer of these little headline grabbing small announcements.
"What we need is the big picture so people know they are getting real help with the cost of living."
Reform UK leader Nigel Farage said the VAT cut was "something that is only allowed because of Brexit".
But he said it would not "address the real issue of the cost of living" and of running a home, because "most houses are heated by oil or gas" and bills were being inflated by green levies.
The End Fuel Poverty Coalition welcomed the reduction, but said it "does not address the scale of what households are facing".
Simon Francis, the group's co-ordinator, said Burnham's government must "go even further" with targeted support for those most in need.
He added: "This breathing space is also not a cure. The only way to bring bills down for good is to change how they are set."

title: Ozempic-maker accuses rival of false advertising
summary: Novo Nordisk has launched legal action accusing Eli Lilly of creating "misleading" ads, a claim its rival denies.
body:
The maker of Wegovy and Ozempic, Novo Nordisk, has launched legal action accusing its arch rival Eli Lilly of false advertising in suggesting its weight-loss drugs perform better.
The Danish company filed a lawsuit in the US on Tuesday claiming Eli Lilly, which makes Mounjaro and Zepbound, deployed ad campaigns to "create the misleading impression that Eli Lilly's medicines are superior".
Novo said its competitor compared the highest approved doses of its medicines for obesity and type-2 diabetes with lower doses of Novo Nordisk's, while omitting newer, higher-dose options.
Eli Lilly hit back at the claims and said it stood "firmly behind our advertising".
"It is truthful, it is transparent, and it is grounded in the most direct scientific evidence available - exactly what patients deserve. We will continue to focus on the science and defend against this lawsuit vigorously," a spokesperson for the company said.
The lawsuit comes as pharmaceutical heavyweights Novo and Eli Lilly are locked in battle to dominate the fast-growing weight-loss drug industry, especially in the US, which analysts have estimated could be worth more than $100bn by 2030.
Novo Nordisk claimed its main competitor in the weight-loss drug business had committed "multiple violations" of federal and state false advertising and unfair competition laws, through its nationwide ad campaigns.
The company said Eli Lilly's current campaigns "intentionally" selected outdated studies comparing Lilly's highest doses against lower doses of Novo Nordisk's medicines.
It said the ads had "deceptively" presented that Eli Lilly's products were superior, but buried or omitted "critical clinical context".
The products being compared incorrectly, according to Novo, were Mounjaro vs. Ozempic and Zepbound vs Wegovy.
"As new and more effective treatment options become available, people deserve accurate information that reflects the latest scientific evidence and helps them make informed care decisions," said John F. Kuckelman, senior vice president and group general counsel for Novo Nordisk.
"Healthcare companies have a responsibility to keep their public claims accurate and current - ineffective, fine-print disclaimers do not fix the misleading impression created by major national campaigns," he said.
Novo said it was seeking a court order requiring Eli Lilly to pull its ads and instead run what it called a "corrective advertising campaign".
It added if Eli Lilly did not voluntarily remove the commercials, it would file a motion in the coming days to seek a preliminary injunction to block them.
But Eli Lilly accused Novo of asking the court to stop the company from communicating the results of a comparison trial it said was "robustly designed" and "the gold standard".

title: Thames Water lenders offer 'golden share' to head off nationalisation
summary: The water supplier's lenders are trying to prevent the company from being taken over by the Burnham government
body:
Thames Water's lenders want to let local authorities have more involvement
Thames Water's main lenders are offering the government a "golden share" and more control for local authorities in a bid to stop the troubled supplier from being nationalised.
The government recently rejected a previous rescue proposal, and the BBC understands the lenders arepreparing a legal challengein case the new Andy Burnham-led government takes the firm into public hands.
The "golden share" would give the government veto powers over major decisions such as mergers.
The lenders are also proposing givinglocal authorities greater involvement in the firm, similar to the relationship between United Utilities and Greater Manchester agreed when Burnham was the city's mayor.
In his first speech as prime minister on Monday, Burnham said he wanted to see greater public control of "life's essentials".
Sources close to the creditors have told the BBC that in the event of full nationalisation they would pursue payment in full of the outstanding debts, as has happened in previous cases, which could leave the government with a multi-billion-pound bill.
The London & Valley Water (L&VW ) consortium of lenders had already proposed a £10bn deal to prevent Thames Water from entering administration. It would involve writing off nearly half of its debt and injecting new cash in return for leniency on future pollution fines.
The deal wasrejected by the government in June, with then-environment secretary Emma Reynolds saying it did not do enough for consumers or the environment.
Sources close to the new deal said the creditors had sweetened it with hundreds of millions in new money on top of the existing offer.
A golden share would give the government veto power over major decisions such as mergers and acquisitions. Golden shares are seen as a way for the government to retain a stake in companies of national significance or importance, such as Royal Mail or Rolls Royce.
Any new proposals would have to be reviewed by Ofwat, the regulator.
A government spokesperson said Thames Water "remains financially stable, but we stand ready for all eventualities, including applying for a Special Administration Regime [SAR] if that were to become necessary".
"The government will always act in the national interest on these issues," they added.
An SAR is a form of temporary nationalisation which would allow the government to recoup some taxpayer cash if Thames was sold again to a private buyer.
L&VW said on Tuesday that the new offer had "material improvements" on the old one, and would benefit customers.
A spokesperson said: "We continue to believe that the L&VW plan is by far the fastest and most reliable route to solving Thames Water's complex problems and improving outcomes for customers and the environment."
They said the new deal "achieves this without any government funding or cost to taxpayers".
Fears first emerged three years ago that Thames Water could collapse and it has recently warned it could run out of cash by November.
If the company does go bust, households will still have drinking water and sewerage services.
Thames Water announced ahosepipe banfor all of its customers on Tuesday morning, citing the UK's "exceptionally warm and dry spring".
The company, which supplies water and wastewater services for 16 million people across London and parts of southern England, was handed a £122.7m fine last year, the biggest ever issued by the industry regulator Ofwat, for breaching rules on sewage spills and shareholder payouts.
Sources close to the creditors have previously told the BBC that in the event of full nationalisation, they would pursue payment in full of the outstanding debts as has happened in previous cases, which could leave the government with a multi-billion-pound bill.

title: Lindt's Easter chocolate sales fall after price hike
summary: The Swiss chocolate maker adjusts its strategy in response to weaker demand after its price rises.
body:
Lindt has partially U-turned on its decision to hike prices after Easter chocolate sales dropped.
The Swiss chocolate maker said a "necessary groupwide" price surge of 11.8% was one of the reasons revenue shrank in the first half of this year, particularly in the UK, Germany, and Switzerland.
It also blamed weaker Easter demand and a drop in tourism from Asia and the Middle East "due to geopolitical uncertainties". In response, it said it has adjusted prices and boosted marketing in certain regions for the second half of the year.
Around Easter, Lindt is known for its chocolate rabbits wrapped in gold-coloured foil and decorated with a red ribbon and bell on their necks.
Overall, the company's sales dipped 0.9%, with European sales down by 2.1%. It added that "performance was impacted by more price-sensitive and mature markets such as Germany, Switzerland and the UK".
By volume, meaning the amount of chocolate sold rather than the money it made, overall sales sank 7.5%. Pre-tax profit fell 1.5%.
Meanwhile, sales of Lindt chocolate in airports decreased "due to ongoing conflicts in the Middle East, and therefore declining passenger traffic".
Lindt said its sales picked up in North America, Australia, China, and Japan, though these countries account for a much smaller slice of its sales than Europe, where it makes over half its revenue.
Lindt chief executive Adalbert Lechner said: "The actions we have initiated focus on volume recovery in the second half of 2026 and lay the foundation to regain volume growth momentum in 2027."
Lindt is not the only chocolate firm which has been putting prices up.
Experts say climate change has led to extreme rainfall and droughts which have decreased cocoa farmers' crops.
This pushes up costs of making chocolate, and companies have chosen different ways to react to this, with some reducingchocolate content or sizes rather than raising prices.
According to the latest official data, theannual rate of chocolate and sweet price rises is 7.9%,external– much higher thanthe general rate of UK inflation at 2.8%,external.

title: Ryanair broken window may have been caused by 'foreign object'
summary: Passenger Ljubisa Karović was partially sucked out of a cabin window on the Boeing aircraft.
body:
Watch: Inside Ryanair plane flying with dislodged window
An incident on a Ryanair flight which saw a passenger partially sucked out of a plane window may have been caused by "foreign object damage" to an engine, said the airline's boss.
Michael O'Leary said an initial investigation into the incident on 10 July between Greece and Germany suggests "it looks like a foreign object damage to the engine on takeoff at Thessaloniki but ... we can't say that definitively".
He insisted that the engine on the 18-year-old Boeing 737 plane had been fully serviced and overhauled in the past two years.
Serbian national Ljubisa Karović'shead and right shoulder were sucked out of the plane's window, leaving him "seriously injured and in shock".
His wife Svetlana Grković Maksimovićtold BBC Serbiathat she and two other passengers held on to his legs for several minutes.
She said: "We pulled him back together. His entire face was deformed and blood was pouring from his nose and mouth."
He was taken to hospital after the plane made an emergency landing.
Passengers told local media they heard "some kind of explosion" on board.
The aircraft was operated by Ryanair's subsidiary Malta Air.
Confirmation that the damage was caused by an external object could affect Ryanair or Boeing's liabilities over the incident.
The US National Transportation Safety Board (NTSB) is investigating. O'Leary said that a draft report would be issued in about 28 days.
'If we die, we die together': Wife of man nearly sucked out of Ryanair plane speaks of ordeal

title: Burnham tells cabinet to tackle living costs at first meeting
summary: The new prime minister chaired his first cabinet after announcing a cut to VAT on household electricity.
body:
Andy Burnham has promised to lead a "cost of living government," as he chaired his first cabinet meeting as prime minister.
He told his newly-assembled top team that he wanted to make living expenses a key priority, and give people a "sense that help is coming" on costs.
It comes after he announced a VAT cut to household energy bills from October, in his first major announcement since taking office.
But his plan to pay for the policy by scrapping Sir Keir Starmer's digital ID scheme led to an early row over whether the plan was properly funded.
Downing Street said the estimated £850m cost of the VAT cut over the rest of the financial year could come from £1.8bn earmarked by the previous government to roll out the digital ID scheme in the next three years.
But Darren Jones, who was among a clutch of Starmer loyalists sacked from the cabinet on Monday, said the ID programme was "unfunded" and Burnham would need to set out where the money would be found.
Opposition parties also seized on the announcement, pointing out the government's budget watchdog has previously said the money to fund digital ID was due to be found from within existing budgets.
"We are only one day in and already Labour are playing fast and loose with the public finances," said Conservative shadow chancellor Sir Mel Stride.
The PM's spokesman insisted the policy would be funded by redirecting "savings" identified by departments to cover the cost of the ID scheme.
Burnham was given a round of applause by his senior ministers earlier, as he opened the first meeting of his new cabinet after appointing his top team on Monday.
Appointments below cabinet rank are expected to follow on Tuesday evening, including Scottish Labour leader Anas Sarwar, who isset to be given a seat in the House of Lords so he can join Burnham's government.
It is thought Sarwar, who rocked Starmer's premiership when hecalled for him to resignin February, will become a business minister.
New rules mean he would have to quit his seat in the Scottish Parliament, although a by-election to replace him will not be required as he was elected on the regional list system.
Monday's wide-ranging reshuffle saw Starmer loyalists such as Rachel Reeves and David Lammy out of government, with Ed Miliband promoted to foreign secretary and Burnham ally Louise Haigh given a big job running the Cabinet Office.
Miatta Fahnbulleh, a Burnham ally promoted into the cabinet as energy secretary, denied he had led a purge of Starmer supporters, telling BBC Newsnight "no one has a right" to be in the cabinet, which contained a "diversity of views".
Speaking to staff at the Treasury, new chancellor John Healey said discipline over tax and spending would be his "first duty", as he recommitted to the tax and spending rules set by his predecessor.
But he added that government would seek to make the "fullest possible use of public investment" within the framework, alongside making the most of private and international investment.
Healey gave an address to Treasury staff after his surprise appointment on Monday
Speaking at the start of the cabinet meeting attended by broadcasters, Burnham said the cost of living would be an early priority, with further measures to be announced in the coming days.
He said he would be asking ministers to come up with suggestions to lower costs to households, adding: "There are things can be done, big and small."
He insisted his government would always show "how we are going to pay for" policies, adding "discipline" on spending would be required.
But he conceded that funding new announcements ahead of his first Budget, where big tax decisions are made, would require ministers to be "prepared to reprioritise" how they allocate their budgets.
This was also reflected in Burnham's first announcement on Monday, a further £340m to tackle rough sleeping over five years, which is expected to be funded within "uncommitted" spending at the housing department.
In a later development on Tuesday, Burnham agreed to allow British military bases to be used for US defensive strikes against Iran - a continuation of the same policy under Sir Keir's government.
Sources stressed bases at Diego Garcia and RAF Fairford would only be used for what the UK deems to be defensive operations.
Sackings, cabinet surprises and policies: Burnham's first 24 hours as PM
Who's in Andy Burnham's cabinet?
VAT to be cut from household electricity bills in October
Three reasons why UK electricity prices are so high
The announcement on energy bills, which will see VAT on household electricity reduced from 5% to zero in England, Wales and Scotland, is estimated to save the typical household about £45 a year.
It willnot apply in Northern Irelanddue to post-Brexit rules agreed by the previous Conservative government, with ministers at Stormont instead offered extra money from the Treasury to use on cost of living measures.
The Conservatives and Reform UK have both previously called for the measure, with Nigel Farage calling it a benefit of Brexit but insisting it would not "address the real issue of the cost of living" and taking aim at green levies on bills.
Meanwhile, further details have been confirmed of Burnham's plan for a new Downing Street unit in Manchester, known as No 10 North.
The government says the unit, expected to be initially set up in a building currently housing intelligence service GCHQ, will be given control of aspects of local economic growth and devolution strategy.
Alongside this, a new Office for the Prime Minister and the Cabinet will be created to ensure departments remain focused on "top strategic priorities", according to astatement announcing the changes,external.
Civil servants working in London have been offered relocation packages to encourage them to move north, it is understood, with Downing Street saying Burnham would try to work from No 10 North "at least one day a week".
Additional reporting by Harry Farley.

title: Sackings, cabinet surprises and policies: Burnham's first 24 hours as PM
summary: The new prime minister enters Number 10 in a flurry of activity - but he is still facing calls for a snap election.
body:
Shortly after Andy Burnham telephoned a number of Keir Starmer's allies to sack them from the cabinet on Monday afternoon, John Healey received the signal to head to Number 10.
Wearing a suit and one of his many red ties, the former defence secretary left parliament alone via a back entrance next to a pub.
"I'm on my way to a meeting," he said with a slight smile as he marched down Whitehall.
Healey's imminent appointment as chancellor was a surprise - to him, to his Labour colleagues, and to the British public.
It was a consequential moment among a flurry of announcements from the new prime minister, includingtoday's cut in VAT on energy bills, that demonstrate the new government's intention to move at breakneck speed - at least for now.
The Conservatives support that tax cut but have questioned its funding.
Five weeks earlier Healey had weakened Keir Starmer's grip on Downing Street by quitting as defence secretary in a row over military spending.
Soon he would be back around the cabinet table alongside two others whose recent resignations had also damaged Starmer - new defence secretary Wes Streeting and new energy secretary Miatta Fahnbulleh.
Were they rewarded for rebelling and hastening Burnham's path to office? Possibly.
But all three are also widely seen as skilled operators taking on complex roles.
Watch: John Healey questioned moments before being made chancellor
John Healey's elevation to the second most important job in government has delighted senior figures in the Ministry of Defence, who are relieved to have someone in the Treasury who is committed to increasing military spending.
"It's brilliant for us," said an insider.
Yet the same MOD source is also relieved Healey is not returning.
"We were dreading him coming back", they said. "He's a difficult person".
Even some senior Conservatives think Healey's appointment as chancellor was clever. "For a number of reasons", explains one former Tory cabinet minister.
"He's not Ed Miliband, he's hard to attack, and he's Treasury-literate." Healey served as a minister in the department for five years during Tony Blair's premiership.
Burnham ally Anneliese Midgeley has the difficult task of trying to keep Labour MPs - including those sacked as ministers - loyal to the government.
Former chief secretary to the prime minister Darren Jones'spublic swipe at Burnham's energy bills policyis a sign not all will play nice.
One Labour MP claims that those sacked by Burnham can be divided into three groups.
"Some went because of their politics, others just weren't good, and then there are those disliked in the parliamentary Labour party," said the MP.
Behind the scenes, many in Westminster are talking about the possibility of Burnham taking advantage of a possible bounce in the opinion polls and calling a snap general election.
Nigel Farage wants one and argues it is necessary since Burnham has no mandate.
But with the Reform leader facing both a by-election and questions over his finances, some in his party worry that if Burnham moves too fast, Farage could miss his one great chance to become PM.
"We are really worried about a snap election," said the Reform source, grinning.
"Which is why we're calling for a snap election."
Miatta Fahnbulleh and John Healey were among the winners in Burnham's reshuffle
Across all the parties at Westminster, plans are being put in place in case a snap contest is called.
"We've started preparing," said a senior Liberal Democrat aide.
Burnham has appeared to rule out heading to the polls.
It would certainly anger some Labour MPs who could lose their seats.
Yet it would also offer the new PM an opportunity to get a fresh mandate for more radical policies, as well as secure his position for a full five year term.
"There is economic pain coming down the horizon", says the former Conservative cabinet minister.
"And it's about how far he [Burnham] gets ahead of it. He faces a choice between going for an election, and a slow decline as we saw with Gordon Brown."
That former PM was dubbed "bottler Brown" in 2007 after considering going to the polls before deciding against it.
Yet Brown got to Number 10 with a mixture of patience, caution, guile and ruthlessness, whereas Burnham's speedy rise was largely due to one major electoral gamble.
After his success in Makerfield and then the Labour leadership contest, will Burnham fancy another throw of the dice?

title: Starmer ally takes swipe at Burnham's first big policy
summary: Sir Keir Starmer's former right hand man Darren Jones says Andy Burnham's VAT cut is unfunded.
body:
Darren Jones was sacked as a minister by new PM Andy Burnham
Whenever there is a reshuffle at the top of government, it is worth keeping an eye and an ear on the dislodged, demoted and rejected former ministers. What do they choose to do and say next?
Andy Burnham has started his first full day in the job witha promise to cut VAT from household electricity billsfrom 1 October.
But already – within hours - he is taking flak from a man who just yesterday was in a senior job in Downing Street working alongside Sir Keir Starmer.
On Monday night, the former Chief Secretary to the Prime Minister, Darren Jones,posted a picture on X of him having a beer,external, captioned "thank you and good night. See you September".
If that was meant to suggest a vow of silence it only lasted a little over 12 hours, because at breakfast time this morning came another post, this one unaccompanied by any picture but loaded with political bite.
"Good news that VAT will be cut on electricity bills," wrote Jones.
"It's a simple way for families to save a few quid, and to mechanically help to keep inflation that little bit lower. But the DigitalID program was unfunded. The government will have to set out how it will pay for its new policies at the budget."
Ouch - that will sting in Downing Street.
Who's in Andy Burnham's cabinet?
And yes, there are four references in the Office for Budget Responsibility'sEconomic and Fiscal Outlook from November,externalof last year that refer to Digital ID and how and where the money will come from to pay for it is not clear.
For example, on page 10, it says "the cost of digital ID cards, for which no specific funding has been identified".
On Page 172 it talks about "the risks around the implementation of digital ID cards for which no explicit provision has been made for its £0.6bn annual cost".
Clearly, this was something of a critique of the funding of the policy Jones was previously an advocate of, so the charge was as relevant to him and Sir Keir Starmer's government about their idea as it is to Andy Burnham and his new idea.
But it is Burnham who is now prime minister and has to set out, as he put it in his first speech in Downing Street, "how we pay for" the policies they are advocating.
And all this just days after Sir Keir Starmer's successor emphasised the need to end "factionalism" within the Labour Party.
At lunchtime, Burnham will gather his cabinet for the first time, after its appointment yesterday became something of a nocturnal affair.
At gone 10pm on Monday night, there was still a minibus full of ministerial wannabes inside Downing Street waiting for their new positions to be confirmed.
One of the later posts to be filled was that of defence secretary, a role taken by Wes Streeting, whose resignation as health secretary two months ago contributed to the draining of Sir Keir's authority as prime minister.
One of the key stories of the reshuffle was a pretty significant cull of the ministers closest too and most loyal to the now former prime minister, Darren Jones among them, but also David Lammy, Peter Kyle, Lord Hermer, Steve Reed and Rachel Reeves – albeit Reeves, I am told, was offered a cabinet post but turned it down.
The question, in the coming days, weeks and months is how much do we hear in public from those banished Starmerites, as Andy Burnham's government sets out its stall.
I hadn't anticipated it starting so soon.

title: Who's in Andy Burnham's cabinet?
summary: An in-depth look at which Labour MPs have been appointed to cabinet roles by new Prime Minister Andy Burnham
body:
Prime Minister Andy Burnham has announced his new cabinet after taking office following the resignation of Sir Keir Starmer.
Tap or click on a person in our interactive guide below to learn more about them and their role in government.
Andy Burnham is the new prime minister, after winning the backing of 379 of Labour's 403 MPs as the best person to replace Sir Keir Starmer.
He has pledged to shift power away from Whitehall, introduce "greater public control" of the water and energy sectors and to build more council houses - while keeping Labour’s election pledge not to raise the main rates of income tax or National Insurance.In one of his first major policy announcements as prime minister, Burnham announced he will remove VAT from electricity bills later this year.Born in Aintree in 1970, Burnham was inspired by the miners’ strike to join the Labour Party in his teens and first became an MP in 2001.The former health secretary had twice failed to become party leader - losing to Ed Miliband in 2010 and Jeremy Corbyn in 2015 - and after leaving Parliament, served as Greater Manchester mayor. He returned as Makerfield MP in June and succeeded Sir Keir Starmer a month later.A life-long Everton fan and talented sportsman, he was part of Labour's Demon Eyes FC football team when Tony Blair was PM, and regularly featured in the Labour MPs vs Lobby journalists match while in Parliament.
In one of his first major policy announcements as prime minister, Burnham announced he will remove VAT from electricity bills later this year.
Born in Aintree in 1970, Burnham was inspired by the miners’ strike to join the Labour Party in his teens and first became an MP in 2001.
The former health secretary had twice failed to become party leader - losing to Ed Miliband in 2010 and Jeremy Corbyn in 2015 - and after leaving Parliament, served as Greater Manchester mayor. He returned as Makerfield MP in June and succeeded Sir Keir Starmer a month later.
A life-long Everton fan and talented sportsman, he was part of Labour's Demon Eyes FC football team when Tony Blair was PM, and regularly featured in the Labour MPs vs Lobby journalists match while in Parliament.
John Healey has returned to the cabinet as chancellor of the exchequer, less than two months after quitting as defence secretary.
A former Treasury minister, Healey recently attacked what he called a "Treasury orthodoxy that's a dead hand on dynamic government" that viewed defence as "a drain rather than a driver of growth".
Senior figures in government say he shares Burnham's ambitions for reindustrialisation and devolution. He now faces the task of making the government's spending plans add up.
It is a surprise appointment for the MP for Rawmarsh and Conisbrough in South Yorkshire, with many speculating the role would go to Ed Miliband or Shabana Mahmood.
Healey was parliamentary private secretary to Chancellor Gordon Brown from 1999 to 2001, before serving as adult skills, Treasury, local government and housing minister between 2001 and 2010.
Louise Haigh has become chancellor of the Duchy of Lancaster, first secretary of state and Cabinet Office minister.
Haigh, one of Andy Burnham’s closest allies, will run the Cabinet Office, the department that supports the prime minister and co-ordinates the government’s policies to deliver its agenda.
After entering parliament as MP for Sheffield Heeley in 2015, Haigh was named the hardest-working new MP in 2016 due to the number of her Commons speeches and questions, and praised by then-Speaker John Bercow for her "terrier-like intensity".
In 2025, the former special constable and Unite union shop steward became the first person to resign from Sir Keir Starmer’s cabinet as transport secretary after it emerged that she had admitted a fraud offence in 2013.
On the backbenches she emerged as a crucial power broker on Labour's "soft left", and was at the heart of the huge rebellion that scuppered the government's welfare cuts in 2025.
Ed Miliband is the new foreign secretary, moving from the energy and net zero brief he has held since Labour returned to power.
He had been touted as a possible chancellor, but opinion was divided between supporters who pointed to his experience in the Treasury, and opponents who distrusted his views, particularly on energy.
Since the election he has spoken out against those urging the government to drop its net zero ambitions and insisted the party's green power transition will help secure social justice and national security.
Miliband has been the MP for Doncaster North since 2005 but has been at the centre of Labour politics for even longer - working for Labour MPs before the 1997 election win and as a special adviser to Gordon Brown when he became chancellor.
He is best known as a former Labour leader - having beaten his brother David, a former foreign secretary, among others, in the leadership contest that followed the party’s 2010 general election defeat - but also co-hosted the Reasons to be Cheerful podcast for several years.
Shabana Mahmood remains home secretary, the position she has held since September 2025.
Mahmood, who has a reputation among Labour MPs as a hardliner on immigration, is responsible for that brief in the role, along with national security and for policing in England and Wales.
When first appointed as home secretary, she announced sweeping changes to the UK’s asylum system, describing the previous system as "out of control".
Her plans include making it harder for migrants already in the UK to settle permanently, which was criticised by more than 100 Labour MPs, peers and union leaders - and former deputy leader Angela Rayner, who is now back in the cabinet, described it as "un-British".
The Birmingham Ladywood MP claims to be the first female Muslim MP - although she was elected in 2010 alongside Yasmin Qureshi and Rushanara Ali - because, she says, her count was the first completed on the night.
Wes Streeting is the new defence secretary, replacing former soldier Dan Jarvis who had only been in the role for a matter of weeks.
Streeting, who resigned as health secretary in May saying he had lost confidence in Sir Keir Starmer as prime minister, eventually decided to back Andy Burnham rather than launch his own leadership bid.
The former National Union of Students president and London councillor is widely seen as one of the party’s most skilled communicators.
He has written an autobiography about growing up in a council flat in London’s East End, visiting his bank robber grandfather in jail, growing up as a gay Christian and about being diagnosed and treated for kidney cancer at the age of 38.
The Ilford North MP has said his upbringing left him "quite cynical" of the role of the state compared with many Labour colleagues, aware of its failings as well as its potential to enable opportunity.
Yvette Cooper remains in cabinet but in a new role as health and social care secretary, having most recently served as foreign secretary in Sir Keir Starmer's government and home secretary before that.
She will face numerous challenges in the role, including getting NHS waiting lists in England down, improving relations with senior doctors and making progress on social care - one of Andy Burnham's priorities.
The former journalist was first elected as an MP in 1997 and went to serve as the first female chief secretary to the Treasury, later becoming work and pensions secretary in the Labour government under Gordon Brown.
Among other firsts in her political career, the Pontefract, Castleford and Knottingley MP became the first minister to take maternity leave, and she was one half of the first married couple in the cabinet - alongside her husband, former education secretary and Strictly Come Dancing contestant, Ed Balls.
Lucy Powell has been appointed as education secretary and says there is "no more important job" than making sure young people have the best education and opportunities they can.
Powell had been leader of the House of Commons - responsible for delivering the government's legislative programme - until she was sacked by Sir Keir Starmer in the reshuffle that followed Angela Rayner's resignation from the cabinet.
She went on to replace Rayner as deputy leader of the party, winning an election of Labour members.
Powell, who joined the Labour Party at 15, has been an MP since winning a by-election in Manchester Central in 2012 and has held several shadow cabinet roles, including stints leading on education, housing and culture.
She led Ed Miliband's successful campaign to become Labour leader in 2010, helping him defeat Andy Burnham among others, and was then Miliband's chief of staff for two years. However, as a Manchester MP she has also worked closely with Burnham for many years.
Pat McFadden remains work and pensions secretary, the role he has held since September 2025.
He took over the department months after the party was forced to U-turn on extensive changes designed to reduce the welfare bill and has pledged to reform the welfare system "in a way that is fair and sustainable".
As the party’s national campaign co-ordinator McFadden was one of the masterminds behind Labour’s historic 2024 election victory, constantly stressing the need for message discipline ahead of the election and warning activists and MPs alike to ignore the opinion polls.
A former adviser to Sir Tony Blair, the Glasgow-born MP has represented the Wolverhampton South East constituency since 2005, serving as a business minister under Gordon Brown and in several shadow ministerial roles since then.
McFadden has also held the post of chancellor of the Duchy of Lancaster, responsible for running the Cabinet Office - the department that supports the prime minister and co-ordinates the government’s policies to deliver its agenda.
Angela Rayner has returned to the cabinet as secretary of state for housing, communities and local government, months after settling her tax affairs with HM Revenue and Customs.
